annotations.label: Mathematical optimization

anchor.snippet: is the selection of a best element, with regard to some criteria

annotations.note: The general notion of selecting a best element is captured by the extremum predicates IsMinOn/IsMaxOn/IsExtrOn, but no overarching 'optimization' definition exists.

annotations.label: Discrete optimization

anchor.snippet: An optimization problem with discrete variables is known as a discrete optimization

annotations.note: ValuedCSP formalizes a broad class of discrete-optimization problems but not the category 'discrete optimization' itself.

annotations.label: Continuous optimization

anchor.snippet: A problem with continuous variables is known as a continuous optimization

annotations.note: Extremum predicates over continuous (topological/normed) domains exist, but 'continuous optimization' is not a defined category.

annotations.label: Optimization problem

anchor.snippet: Such a formulation is called an optimization problem or a mathematical programming problem

annotations.note: IsMinOn/IsMaxOn formalize minimizing/maximizing a function on a set, but there is no general 'optimization problem' structure.

annotations.label: Reduction to minimization

anchor.snippet: it suffices to solve only minimization problems

annotations.note: IsMaxOn.neg (with IsMinOn.neg) formalizes the negation duality underlying reduction of maximization to minimization, not the methodological claim itself.

annotations.label: Search space / choice set

anchor.snippet: is called the search space or the choice set

annotations.note: The feasible set is just the ambient Set in IsMinOn; there is no dedicated 'search space' concept.

annotations.label: Objective function

anchor.snippet: is variously called an objective function , criterion function

annotations.note: The objective is just the function argument of IsMinOn; no separate 'objective function' definition exists.

annotations.label: Optimal solution

anchor.snippet: is called an optimal solution

annotations.note: IsMinOn f s a (dually IsMaxOn) is exactly the notion of a point that is an optimal solution over the feasible set s.

annotations.label: Local minimum

anchor.snippet: A local minimum x * is defined as an element for which there exists some

annotations.note: IsLocalMin f a means f a ≤ f x in a neighborhood of a, exactly the local-minimum definition.

annotations.label: Global minimum

anchor.snippet: a global minimum is at least as good as every feasible element

annotations.note: IsMinOn f s a means f a ≤ f x for all feasible x ∈ s, i.e. a global minimum on s.

annotations.label: Convex local minimum is global

anchor.snippet: if there is a local minimum that is interior

annotations.note: This lemma proves any local minimum of a convex function is a global minimum.

annotations.label: Minimum of x^2+1

anchor.section: Minimum and maximum value of a function
anchor.snippet: The minimum value in this case is 1, occurring at

annotations.note: This specific worked example is not a Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Unbounded maximum of 2x

anchor.section: Minimum and maximum value of a function
anchor.snippet: there is no such maximum as the objective function is unbounded

annotations.note: This illustrative example of an unbounded objective is not formalized as a declaration.

annotations.label: argmin of x^2+1

anchor.snippet: In this case, the answer is x = −1 , since x = 0 is infeasible

annotations.note: This specific argmin example is not a Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: argmax of x cos y

anchor.snippet: In this case, the solutions are the pairs of the form

annotations.note: This specific argmax example is not a Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: arg min and arg max

anchor.snippet: stand for argument of the minimum and argument of the maximum

annotations.note: IsMinOn/IsMaxOn capture being a minimizer/maximizer, but there is no general continuous-optimization argmin/argmax operator.

annotations.label: Convex programming

anchor.snippet: Convex programming studies the case when the objective function is convex

annotations.note: ConvexOn and associated convex-optimization lemmas exist, but 'convex programming' as a problem class is not defined.

annotations.label: Linear programming

anchor.snippet: studies the case in which the objective function f is linear

annotations.note: Farkas' lemma / cone duality underlies LP duality, but linear programs and LP strong duality are an explicit TODO and not defined.

annotations.label: Second-order cone programming

anchor.snippet: Second-order cone programming (SOCP) is a convex program

annotations.note: No second-order cone program is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Semidefinite programming

anchor.snippet: Semidefinite programming (SDP) is a subfield of convex optimization

annotations.note: Mathlib has positive-semidefinite matrices but no semidefinite-programming problem or duality.

annotations.label: Conic programming

anchor.snippet: Conic programming is a general form of convex programming

annotations.note: Convex/proper cones and cone duality (Farkas) are formalized, but no conic-program optimization problem is defined.

annotations.label: Geometric programming

anchor.snippet: Geometric programming is a technique whereby objective and inequality constraints expressed as posynomials

annotations.note: Geometric programming and posynomials are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Integer programming

anchor.snippet: Integer programming studies linear programs in which some or all variables are constrained to take on integer values

annotations.note: Integer programs are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quadratic programming

anchor.snippet: Quadratic programming allows the objective function to have quadratic terms

annotations.note: Quadratic programming is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fractional programming

anchor.snippet: Fractional programming studies optimization of ratios of two nonlinear functions

annotations.note: Fractional programming is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nonlinear programming

anchor.snippet: Nonlinear programming studies the general case in which the objective function or the constraints or both contain nonlinear parts

annotations.note: Nonlinear programming as a problem class is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stochastic programming

anchor.snippet: Stochastic programming studies the case in which some of the constraints or parameters depend on random variables

annotations.note: Stochastic programming is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Robust optimization

anchor.snippet: Robust optimization is, like stochastic programming, an attempt to capture uncertainty

annotations.note: Robust optimization is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Combinatorial optimization

anchor.snippet: Combinatorial optimization is concerned with problems where the set of feasible solutions is discrete

annotations.note: ValuedCSP formalizes a class of discrete/combinatorial optimization problems but not the field in general.

annotations.label: Stochastic optimization

anchor.snippet: Stochastic optimization is used with random (noisy) function measurements

annotations.note: Stochastic optimization is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Infinite-dimensional optimization

anchor.snippet: Infinite-dimensional optimization studies the case when the set of feasible solutions is a subset of an infinite- dimensional space

annotations.note: No infinite-dimensional optimization framework is formalized, though extremum predicates apply to infinite-dimensional spaces.

annotations.label: Heuristics and metaheuristics

anchor.snippet: Heuristics and metaheuristics make few or no assumptions about the problem being optimized

annotations.note: Heuristics and metaheuristics are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Constraint satisfaction

anchor.snippet: Constraint satisfaction studies the case in which the objective function f is constant

annotations.note: ValuedCSP generalizes constraint-satisfaction problems (crisp CSP being the constant-cost case) but the pure CSP feasibility problem is not separately defined.

annotations.label: Disjunctive programming

anchor.snippet: Disjunctive programming is used where at least one constraint must be satisfied but not all

annotations.note: Disjunctive programming is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Space mapping

anchor.snippet: Space mapping is a concept for modeling and optimization of an engineering system

annotations.note: Space mapping is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Calculus of variations

anchor.snippet: Calculus of variations is concerned with finding the best way to achieve some goal

annotations.note: The calculus of variations (e.g. Euler–Lagrange equations) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Optimal control theory

anchor.snippet: Optimal control theory is a generalization of the calculus of variations which introduces control policies

annotations.note: Optimal control theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dynamic programming

anchor.snippet: Dynamic programming is the approach to solve the stochastic optimization problem

annotations.note: Dynamic programming for optimization is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bellman equation

anchor.snippet: is called the Bellman equation

annotations.note: The Bellman equation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mathematical programming with equilibrium constraints

anchor.snippet: Mathematical programming with equilibrium constraints is where the constraints include variational inequalities

annotations.note: MPEC with variational-inequality constraints is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pareto set

anchor.snippet: is known as the Pareto set

annotations.note: The Pareto set is not formalized in Mathlib (only the unrelated Pareto probability distribution exists).

annotations.label: Pareto frontier

anchor.snippet: is known as the Pareto frontier

annotations.note: The Pareto frontier is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pareto optimal

anchor.snippet: A design is judged to be "Pareto optimal"

annotations.note: Pareto optimality is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Vector optimization

anchor.snippet: have been generalized further into vector optimization problems

annotations.note: Vector optimization is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multi-modal optimization

anchor.snippet: Optimization problems are often multi-modal; that is, they possess multiple good solutions

annotations.note: Multi-modal/global optimization is not formalized as a concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Feasibility problem

anchor.snippet: is just the problem of finding any feasible solution at all without regard to objective value

annotations.note: The feasibility problem as an optimization concept is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extreme value theorem (Weierstrass)

anchor.snippet: states that a continuous real-valued function on a compact set attains its maximum and minimum value

annotations.note: IsCompact.exists_isMinOn (and exists_isMaxOn) is the extreme value theorem, generalized to continuous functions into a conditionally complete linear order.

annotations.label: Semicontinuous functions attain extrema

anchor.snippet: a lower semi-continuous function on a compact set attains its minimum

annotations.note: This states a lower semicontinuous function attains its minimum on a nonempty compact set (dually for upper semicontinuous and maxima).

annotations.label: Fermat's stationary point theorem

anchor.snippet: states that optima of unconstrained problems are found at stationary points

annotations.note: IsLocalExtr.hasDerivAt_eq_zero (and IsLocalMin.fderiv_eq_zero) proves the derivative vanishes at an interior local extremum.

annotations.label: Lagrange multiplier method

anchor.snippet: Optima of equality-constrained problems can be found by the Lagrange multiplier method

annotations.note: This file formalizes the Lagrange multipliers method for equality-constrained local extrema.

annotations.label: Karush–Kuhn–Tucker conditions

anchor.snippet: can be found using the ' Karush–Kuhn–Tucker conditions '

annotations.note: KKT conditions are an explicit TODO in the LagrangeMultipliers file and are not formalized.

annotations.label: Second-order sufficient conditions

anchor.snippet: the satisfaction of the second-order conditions as well is sufficient to establish at least local optimality

annotations.note: The general second-order (Hessian) sufficient condition for local optimality is not formalized.

annotations.label: Envelope theorem

anchor.snippet: The envelope theorem describes how the value of an optimal solution changes when an underlying parameter changes

annotations.note: The envelope theorem is listed as a wanted theorem in docs/1000.yaml with no Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Maximum theorem (Berge)

anchor.snippet: The maximum theorem of Claude Berge (1963) describes the continuity of an optimal solution

annotations.note: Berge's maximum theorem is listed as wanted in docs/1000.yaml with no Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Zero subgradient certifies local minimum

anchor.snippet: a zero subgradient certifies that a local minimum has been found

annotations.note: Subgradients are not defined in Mathlib, so the zero-subgradient optimality certificate is not formalized.

annotations.label: Hessian definiteness classification

anchor.snippet: If the Hessian is positive definite at a critical point, then the point is a local minimum

annotations.note: The second-derivative/Hessian-definiteness test for classifying critical points is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Convex local minimum is global

anchor.snippet: When the objective function is a convex function , then any local minimum will also be a global minimum

annotations.note: This lemma proves any local minimum of a convex function is a global minimum.

annotations.label: Heuristic

anchor.snippet: A heuristic is any algorithm which is not guaranteed (mathematically) to find the solution

annotations.note: The notion of a heuristic algorithm is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Utility maximization and expenditure minimization

anchor.snippet: the utility maximization problem and its dual problem , the expenditure minimization problem , are economic optimization problems

annotations.note: Utility maximization and expenditure minimization economic problems are not formalized in Mathlib.
